speaker: interviewer
text: Thursday, December the 7th, 1995. I'm Sharon Tyler. I'm interviewing Trude Levi. We're in London, England and this is tape five. You were telling the story before.

speaker: interviewee
text: In camp, we quite often were called out to do voluntary work. Now, if you went as a volunteer, then there was a chance of getting an extra soup. If you didn't, then you got chased out and got treated quite badly. Our room-- a roommate of mine, who was also the-- the head of our room, and whom I was very, very fond of, and myself very often all volunteered for work. And we got the soup and we usually gave it to the two kids whom we had in our room when we brought it back. This-- and one day we were called out and we had-- it was winter, and we had to bury-- well, it wasn't winter yet, but it was already icy and cold because it was in October, Or definitely by the end of September, it must have been. We were-- were asked, and we had to bury-- dig-- dig a grave-- and I remember that the-- the ground was frozen-- dig a grave for the Commandant's dog. And the Commandant was standing there while we were doing it the whole time. Now, from that moment on I-- that I buried the Commandant's dog, whenever he saw me, he greeted me. Now during my time in camp, I always said that I don't know German. I denied that I understand German, that I know German. I-- and-- and that's how it was accepted that I don't know German. Now, Willi was called away into-- to Buchenwald for a meeting for a couple of days. And during this couple of days, we did not get our bread. When Willi came back, we got our bread again. And a day or two later, I was standing next to two SS women, who were giggling and laughing and talking about it, how they sold the bread during those two days. How they sold our bread and how they got away with it. I don't know whether I gave myself away with my facial expression or what, but the one said, oh, it doesn't matter. She doesn't understand German, anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they had an after thought and they went into my room and they asked one of my roommates, who was a pretty nasty individual, and said, do you-- does she really not understand German? She said, what are you talking of? It's her mother tongue. She only makes as if she doesn't understand German. She speaks fluent German. Which wasn't a very nice thing to do, but anyway, she did it. And then the next day, they came, or a couple of days later, they came and they said that I must go to the appellplatz, to the zahlappell, there's a zahlappell. And only me, they called me out. And I stood there and, at first, there was no one. And then started-- many others started coming. And we said, what's up? And somebody said, oh, they are saying they are taking us to some lighter work into a paper factory.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- and I-- by that time, I was pretty weak already and pretty miserable and fed up and-- and of course, I would have loved to go into because I will tell about my work at that time. Would have loved to go to somewhere where the work would have been easier. So-- so I stood there and then, when there were lots of us, I didn't know how many, the camp Commandant came with the camp doctor, Luciana Nissim, who was, by the way, an Italian, and who didn't speak Hungarian. But she spoke French, and because she spoke French, we became great friends. We are great friends up till today. And she was an absolutely lovely person. And what happened, we were-- and they counted us. The Commandant counted us and we were 208. Now, that week, in the books of Luciana, people who came to her surgery she always had to write down. There were 206, not 208. And the Commandant had been told that those who reported sick that week had to be taken away. But there were only 206, not 208. So he came and took me out and took another person out. And I have got a list to prove it, where my number is rewritten where my name should be.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were the two-- you and the person-- the other person-- were you people who both hadn't been to the-- surgery--

speaker: interviewee
text: Who hadn't been-- hadn't been to the surgery. I actually have been, but I have not been to the surgery because I was very friendly with her. If I was ill, I just told her. I mean, if I felt bad, I just told her, when I anyway was with her. So I was, in that way, very lucky I wasn't on the list. So that-- and that's how-- what saved my life.
